title: Cheat Sheets
description: Concise revision prompts for LLMs, prompting, embeddings, RAG, agents, LLMOps, statistics, ML, and system design.

questions.title: Give a concise LLM fundamentals cheat sheet.
questions.scenario: A candidate doing final revision before AI interviews runs compressed study guide for LLMs, RAG, agents, ML, statistics, and system design. A recent review found that revision memorizes definitions but misses when and why techniques work in production; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for LLM fundamentals cheat sheet measured by recall speed, answer structure, example quality, and gap closure.
questions.expectedAnswer: LLM fundamentals cheat sheet is important because it changes how the system is designed, measured, and trusted in production. In compressed study guide for LLMs, RAG, agents, ML, statistics, and system design, the practical approach is model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from core concepts, failure modes, metrics, examples, and common tradeoffs, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using OpenAI/Azure OpenAI, vLLM, LiteLLM, model gateways, and eval datasets, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is fluent output that hides uncertainty, cost, or latency problems; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use recall speed, answer structure, example quality, and gap closure, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: The scenario points to fluent output that hides uncertainty, cost, or latency problems. LLM fundamentals cheat sheet addresses that risk through model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability, so the discussion should start with the observed production failure rather than a textbook definition. The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using recall speed, answer structure, example quality, and gap closure.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from core concepts, failure modes, metrics, examples, and common tradeoffs. Implement the change with OpenAI/Azure OpenAI, vLLM, LiteLLM, model gateways, and eval datasets, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in compressed study guide for LLMs, RAG, agents, ML, statistics, and system design, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. The improved path adds model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability; it records the evidence, score, trace, or review status that supports the output. The final result is tied to validated signals instead of model confidence or notebook accuracy alone.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: The technique adds engineering overhead, evaluation work, and usually some latency or infrastructure cost. It is worthwhile when the failure affects trust, safety, revenue, or repeated manual effort. It is not worth overengineering for rare, low-risk tasks where a simple baseline already meets the target. Monitor recall speed, answer structure, example quality, and gap closure so the team can tell whether the extra complexity pays for itself.

relatedQuestions.title: Give a concise RAG cheat sheet.

commonMistakes: Explaining LLM fundamentals cheat sheet as a memorized definition without tying it to a production failure mode.
Skipping measurement and relying on anecdotal demo quality instead of recall speed, answer structure, example quality, and gap closure.
Ignoring the main risk: fluent output that hides uncertainty, cost, or latency problems.
Not discussing ownership, rollout, monitoring, and rollback when the approach fails in production.
followUpQuestions: What metric would prove that LLM fundamentals cheat sheet improved the system rather than just changed it?
What failure mode would you expect after deploying LLM fundamentals cheat sheet, and how would you detect it?
How would your answer change if latency, cost, or compliance became the primary constraint?
questions.realWorldExample: A candidate doing final revision before AI interviews applied LLM fundamentals cheat sheet in compressed study guide for LLMs, RAG, agents, ML, statistics, and system design. The baseline suffered from fluent output that hides uncertainty, cost, or latency problems, causing roughly 25% of reviewed cases to require manual correction. The team implemented model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability using OpenAI/Azure OpenAI, vLLM, LiteLLM, model gateways, and eval datasets, then evaluated the change against recall speed, answer structure, example quality, and gap closure. After a limited rollout, manual corrections dropped by 12-18 percentage points and p95 latency stayed within the agreed operating budget.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate gives a concrete mechanism for LLM fundamentals cheat sheet, names the data and metrics needed to validate it, and explains the operational risk. A great candidate also discusses rollout, observability, ownership, and when the added complexity is not justified.

questions.title: Give a concise RAG cheat sheet.
questions.scenario: A candidate doing final revision before AI interviews runs compressed study guide for LLMs, RAG, agents, ML, statistics, and system design. A recent review found that revision memorizes definitions but misses when and why techniques work in production; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for RAG cheat sheet measured by recall speed, answer structure, example quality, and gap closure.
questions.expectedAnswer: RAG cheat sheet is important because it changes how the system is designed, measured, and trusted in production. In compressed study guide for LLMs, RAG, agents, ML, statistics, and system design, the practical approach is retrieval grounding with chunking, hybrid search, reranking, citations, and faithfulness checks. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from core concepts, failure modes, metrics, examples, and common tradeoffs, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using Milvus or pgvector, OpenSearch BM25, a cross-encoder reranker, and Ragas-style evaluation, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is retrieving plausible but unsupported context; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use recall speed, answer structure, example quality, and gap closure, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

questions.title: Give a concise agents cheat sheet.

A recent review found that revision memorizes definitions but misses when and why techniques work in production; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for agents cheat sheet measured by recall speed, answer structure, example quality, and gap closure.
questions.expectedAnswer: agents cheat sheet is important because it changes how the system is designed, measured, and trusted in production. In compressed study guide for LLMs, RAG, agents, ML, statistics, and system design, the practical approach is bounded planning, typed tool calls, state management, approval gates, and trace-based evaluation. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from core concepts, failure modes, metrics, examples, and common tradeoffs, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using LangGraph, tool schemas, ServiceNow/Jira APIs, and LangSmith traces, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is unbounded autonomy, tool misuse, loops, and hidden state corruption; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use recall speed, answer structure, example quality, and gap closure, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

questions.title: Give a concise statistics and ML cheat sheet.

questions.expectedAnswer: statistics and ML cheat sheet is important because it changes how the system is designed, measured, and trusted in production. In compressed study guide for LLMs, RAG, agents, ML, statistics, and system design, the practical approach is statistical reasoning with hypothesis design, uncertainty, sample size, and guardrail metrics. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from core concepts, failure modes, metrics, examples, and common tradeoffs, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using SQL, scipy, statsmodels, experiment platforms, and power analysis, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is claiming causal impact from noisy or biased data; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use recall speed, answer structure, example quality, and gap closure, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

questions.title: How would you explain embeddings revision in a Cheat Sheets interview?
questions.scenario: A candidate doing final revision before AI interviews runs compressed study guide for LLMs, RAG, agents, ML, statistics, and system design. A recent review found that revision memorizes definitions but misses when and why techniques work in production; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for embeddings revision measured by recall speed, answer structure, example quality, and gap closure.
questions.expectedAnswer: embeddings revision is best explained by starting from the failure users are seeing and then naming the system change that removes that failure. In compressed study guide for LLMs, RAG, agents, ML, statistics, and system design, the practical approach is embedding model selection, ANN indexing, metadata filtering, and reranking. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from core concepts, failure modes, metrics, examples, and common tradeoffs, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using Milvus, Pinecone, pgvector, HNSW/IVF indexes, and NDCG@10 evaluation, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is semantic matches that ignore permissions, exact terms, or embedding-version drift; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use recall speed, answer structure, example quality, and gap closure, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

questions.expectedAnswer: statistics revision belongs in the architecture discussion because it affects ownership boundaries, controls, observability, and rollout.
